description: Professional clean-code guidance covering simplicity, maintainability, naming, duplication control, i18n, and privacy compliance. Includes references to Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Refactoring by Martin Fowler, and Google Style Guides.
query_hint: Recall this domain for implementation quality, refactors, naming, duplication, simplicity, i18n, and LGPD/GDPR-sensitive code decisions. Also recall for Clean Code, Refactoring, and Google Style Guides.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Prefer SOLID boundaries
items.body: Classes, modules, and services must have focused, single responsibilities. Enforce clear contracts using dependency inversion, Liskov substitution rules, and interface segregation. Separate domain logic from external dependencies (DB, APIs, GUI) to allow clean testing and independent evolution. Avoid creating 'God' classes or bloated interfaces that couple unrelated actors.
items.trigger: creating or refactoring modules, services, classes, traits, or interfaces
items.remediation: Split mixed responsibilities into separate classes/functions. Inject dependencies explicitly via constructors/initializers rather than instantiating them inline. Keep interfaces small and specific so that callers do not depend on methods they do not use.

items.title: Apply KISS and YAGNI before abstraction
items.body: Always choose the simplest implementation that satisfies current, validated requirements. Do not introduce speculative interfaces, complex configuration layers, multi-tenant schemas, or generic extension points until there is a concrete, active second use-case that demands it. Over-engineering increases cognitive load and slows down refactoring.
items.trigger: adding abstractions, configuration systems, service layers, or extensibility points
items.remediation: Inline the logic directly or keep the implementation concrete. Avoid writing generic code or base classes when a simple, explicit function handles the exact case. Do not add fields, methods, or configurations 'just in case'.

items.title: Use DRY for knowledge, not coincidence
items.body: Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY) applies to business rules, algorithms, and logical invariants, not identical-looking markup or incidental code duplication. Extracting shared code just because two blocks look similar can lead to high coupling, where changes to one business flow break another unrelated flow.
items.trigger: reviewing duplicated code or proposing a shared helper
items.remediation: Check if the duplication represents a shared business invariant (knowledge). If yes, extract it to a unified helper. If not (coincidental representation), keep them separate to allow them to evolve independently. Prefer clean, readable duplication over a wrong abstraction.

items.title: Name variables and functions by intent
items.body: Symbols must clearly convey domain intent, units, side-effects, and lifecycle. Avoid names containing generic technical terms like 'data', 'item', 'value', 'handler', 'processor', or 'manager' when a precise business domain term is available. Names should be long enough to be self-explanatory but short enough to be readable.
items.trigger: writing variables, functions, modules, or public APIs
items.remediation: Rename symbols so that a reader can understand what the entity represents and what a function accomplishes without reading its body. Include units in names where relevant (e.g., 'timeout_ms' instead of 'timeout').

items.title: Respect LGPD and GDPR data minimization
items.body: Under GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) and LGPD Art. 6(III), systems must collect, store, and process only personal data that is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the declared purposes. Avoid collecting sensitive attributes (e.g., health, union status, origin) unless explicitly justified. Enforce storage limitations by implementing automatic deletion/anonymization schedules.
items.trigger: handling personal data, telemetry, logs, exports, analytics, or user profiles
items.remediation: Audit the data schema. Remove columns or fields that do not have a documented processing purpose. Implement field-level encryption (AES-256) for PII (Personally Identifiable Information) columns and ensure logs redact sensitive fields automatically.

items.title: Externalize user-facing text for i18n
items.body: All user-facing strings, validation errors, dates, numbers, currencies, and layout labels must be externalized using localized translation resources instead of hardcoding text inline. Grammar rules, pluralization forms, and text direction (RTL) must be handled dynamically through the internationalization layer to prevent broken UI layouts in other locales.
items.trigger: adding UI copy, API-facing messages, emails, notifications, errors, placeholders, labels, or formatted dates and numbers
items.remediation: Move inline strings to language files (e.g., JSON/YAML locales). Use locale-aware formatting libraries for currency, date, and numbers. Do not build messages using string concatenation, as grammatical structure varies by language; use templating placeholders instead.

items.title: Run the clean-code review checklist
items.body: Before completing a pull request or code generation task, perform a structured code quality review: 1) Verify SOLID bounds. 2) Ensure no over-engineering (KISS/YAGNI). 3) Confirm DRY is used only for shared domain logic. 4) Apply Robert Martin's meaningful naming rules (intention-revealing, no abbreviations). 5) Follow Martin Fowler's Refactoring process (never mix refactoring and adding features; wear one hat at a time). 6) Identify and eliminate code smells (Feature Envy, Data Clumps). 7) Check for i18n compliance and PII protection.
items.trigger: reviewing a pull request, refactor, generated code, or marketplace-applied domain guidance

items.title: Use mutexes to protect shared mutable state — never skip unlock
items.body: A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) ensures that only one thread or goroutine can access a critical section at a time. Violating this guarantee causes data races, memory corruption, and non-deterministic crashes. Rules: 1) Always acquire and release a lock in the same lexical scope or use RAII guards (Rust `MutexGuard`, C++ `std::lock_guard`, Java `try-finally`). 2) Never hold two mutexes simultaneously unless lock ordering is globally enforced — acquiring locks in inconsistent orders causes deadlock. 3) Minimize the critical section size: lock, operate on shared state, unlock. Do not call blocking I/O, network calls, or user callbacks while holding a mutex. 4) Prefer read-write locks (`RwLock`) when reads vastly outnumber writes. 5) Detect deadlock potential with static analysis tools (`ThreadSanitizer`, `cargo-geiger`, or Helgrind) during CI.
items.trigger: implementing concurrent data access, shared state, caches, counters, queues, or multi-threaded services
items.remediation: Audit all mutex lock sites. Establish a canonical lock acquisition order documented in the module header. Replace any manual lock/unlock pairs with RAII guards. Extract critical sections into dedicated, short-lived functions. If a deadlock is suspected, use a timeout-aware `try_lock` variant and log the contention for investigation.

items.title: Use semaphores for resource-pool and producer-consumer coordination
items.body: A semaphore is a signaling mechanism that limits the number of concurrent accessors to a bounded resource (e.g., DB connection pool, file handles, HTTP request slots). Unlike mutexes, they do not enforce mutual exclusion — they track availability. Two core patterns: 1) Counting Semaphore: initialized to N, allows N concurrent operations. Use for connection pools, download concurrency limits, or worker-thread caps. 2) Binary Semaphore: initialized to 1, signals between two parties (producer notifies consumer). Prefer structured channels or async primitives (Go channels, Rust `tokio::sync::Semaphore`, Java `Semaphore`) over raw OS semaphores. Avoid sleeping in a spin-wait loop to check for permits — use `acquire()` with async/await or blocking where appropriate.
items.trigger: implementing connection pools, rate limiters, worker queues, producer-consumer pipelines, or concurrent download managers
items.remediation: Replace busy-wait loops with semaphore acquire calls. Verify the semaphore count matches the actual resource pool capacity. Ensure `release()` is always called in a finally block or RAII destructor even on error paths — leaked permits will starve the pool. For async systems, prefer structured concurrency libraries over raw semaphore primitives.

items.title: Choose sorting algorithms by data profile and stability requirements
items.body: Sorting algorithm selection directly impacts latency, memory usage, and correctness for ordered output. Key trade-offs: 1) Quick Sort — average O(n log n) time, O(log n) stack space. Fastest in practice for random data due to cache locality. Worst case O(n²) on already-sorted or adversarial input; mitigate with randomized pivot or median-of-three pivot. Unstable — do not use when preserving equal-element order matters. 2) Merge Sort — guaranteed O(n log n) worst case, O(n) auxiliary space. Stable — preserves relative order of equal elements. Preferred for linked lists, external sorting (datasets larger than RAM), and when stability is required. 3) Heap Sort — guaranteed O(n log n) worst case, O(1) in-place auxiliary space. Unstable. Poor cache locality makes it slower than Quick Sort in practice despite the same asymptotic complexity. Use when worst-case guarantees matter and extra memory is prohibited (embedded systems). 4) Prefer language-provided sort (e.g., `slice::sort` in Rust, `Arrays.sort` in Java, `sorted()` in Python) which are typically hybrid Timsort or introsort, providing best-of-all-worlds performance. Only implement a custom sort when benchmarks prove a clear improvement.
items.trigger: implementing ordering, ranking, priority queues, leaderboards, search result ranking, or any custom sort logic
items.remediation: Replace custom comparison-based sorting with the standard library sort unless profiling shows it is a bottleneck. If stability is required, use a stable sort variant. If memory is constrained, use Heap Sort. Always test sorting with edge cases: empty collection, single element, all-equal elements, reverse-sorted input, and very large datasets.

items.title: Run the concurrency correctness checklist
items.body: Before shipping any concurrent or parallel code, execute this checklist: 1) DATA RACES — Is every shared mutable variable protected by a mutex, RwLock, or atomic? 2) DEADLOCK — Are all lock acquisitions in a globally consistent order? Are locks never held across blocking I/O? 3) STARVATION — Can a semaphore pool be exhausted and never released on error paths? 4) LIVELOCK — Can two threads indefinitely retry without making progress? 5) VISIBILITY — Are memory ordering semantics correct for atomics (Acquire/Release vs. SeqCst)? 6) RESOURCE LEAK — Is every acquired lock, permit, or connection released in all code paths (error, panic, timeout)? 7) ORDERING CORRECTNESS — If output order matters, is a stable sort used? Is the sort key deterministic and total-ordering compliant?
items.trigger: reviewing multi-threaded code, async pipelines, shared cache implementations, concurrent queues, or any code using mutex, semaphore, channel, or parallel sort

items.title: Apply privacy by design and default
items.body: GDPR Art. 25 and LGPD Art. 46 mandate that privacy-enhancing safeguards must be integrated into the system architecture from the earliest design stages. Settings must default to the highest level of privacy (e.g., tracking opted-out, optional data fields empty, minimal profile visibility, and auto-delete enabled) rather than requiring user opt-out.
items.trigger: designing forms, telemetry, logs, exports, analytics, consent, account data, or personalization
items.remediation: Implement an explicit consent-gating mechanism. Ensure third-party analytics and marketing SDKs are disabled by default and only loaded after receiving user consent. Validate that personal data deleted by a user is also deleted from backups and third-party subprocessors within legal time limits.

items.title: Justify sequential vs parallel execution by necessity, cost, and complexity
items.body: Before introducing parallel or concurrent execution, perform a three-axis evaluation: 1) NECESSITY — Does the workload contain independent, CPU-bound or I/O-bound units that are actually blocked by sequential execution? Measure with profiling, not assumption. 2) COST — Parallelism adds synchronization overhead (lock contention, context switches, cache invalidation, atomic operations), increased memory footprint (one stack per thread), and non-deterministic failure modes. The speedup benefit must outweigh these costs using Amdahl's Law analysis: speedup is bounded by the serial fraction of the workload. 3) COMPLEXITY — Concurrent code is harder to read, test, and debug. Race conditions and deadlocks are notoriously difficult to reproduce. Prefer structured concurrency primitives (async/await with structured cancellation, thread pools, parallel iterators like Rayon in Rust or ParallelStream in Java) over manual thread management. Sequential code must be the default; parallelism is an optimization that requires explicit justification and benchmarks.
items.trigger: adding threads, goroutines, async tasks, parallel iterators, background workers, thread pools, or any concurrent data pipeline
items.remediation: Profile the sequential version first. Identify the bottleneck using a profiler (e.g., `perf`, `async-profiler`, `tokio-console`). Apply parallelism only to the identified bottleneck. Prefer data-parallel operations (parallel map/fold over collections) over manually spawning threads. Document the justification (measured speedup, workload profile) in a code comment adjacent to the parallelism boundary.

description: Architecture decision guidance for selecting patterns by context, complexity, team maturity, and operational constraints. Includes principles from Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture by Martin Fowler, and System Design Interview by Alex Xu.
query_hint: Recall this domain before choosing architectural style, service boundaries, modularization, or teacher-mode trade-off evaluation. Also recall for Enterprise Application Architecture and System Design.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Use teacher mode for architecture evaluation
items.body: Assess architecture proposals by systematically walking through: 1) Alex Xu's System Design Framework: establish design scope, propose high-level design, deep-dive into database/API contracts, and wrap up with scaling bottlenecks. 2) Evaluate structural patterns (Fowler's Domain Model, Active Record, Transaction Script). 3) Analyze team topology (Conway's Law, domain boundaries). 4) Perform trade-off analysis of at least two alternative patterns. 5) Plan failure modes and disaster recovery.
items.trigger: choosing or reviewing architecture

items.title: Match architecture to context and complexity
items.body: Choose the simplest architectural style that successfully supports current operational and organizational scales. Do not adopt complex distributed architectures (e.g., event sourcing, microservices, service meshes) solely because they are industry trends. High architecture complexity incurs high maintenance costs, deployment friction, and cognitive overhead.
items.trigger: choosing between monolith, modular monolith, microservices, serverless, event-driven, or hexagonal designs
items.remediation: Write a brief context document. Benchmark the throughput and availability requirements. Compare the operational cost of managing a single deployment vs. multiple distributed services. If the current team is under 15 developers, default to a modular monolith.

items.title: Prefer modular monoliths until distribution is justified
items.body: A modular monolith organizes code into strict, bounded contexts inside a single deployable unit. It keeps domain boundaries clean using compiler checks or internal visibility modifiers, allowing modular design while keeping data consistency trivial (single DB transactions) and avoiding network overhead.
items.trigger: starting a new backend or splitting an existing system
items.remediation: Enforce package-level or assembly-level encapsulation. Prevent direct cross-module database joins; instead, access data via defined public interfaces of other modules. Use an internal event bus or direct method calls for cross-boundary communication.

items.title: Use hexagonal architecture to protect domain logic
items.body: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters) isolates the core business logic from databases, UI, web frameworks, messaging systems, and external APIs. The domain core must define incoming and outgoing interfaces (ports), and external infrastructure must implement adapters that map details to these ports. This makes the business logic highly testable and independent of framework version upgrades.
items.trigger: domain logic is coupled to infrastructure or framework APIs
items.remediation: Define interfaces (Ports) in your domain layer for database repositories, external notifications, and system time. Implement these interfaces in infrastructure modules (Adapters). Keep domain models free of framework annotations, decorators, and database-specific libraries.

items.title: Use microservices only with operational readiness
items.body: Microservices are appropriate when different domains need separate release cycles, have vastly different scaling characteristics, or are owned by independent teams. They require high operational maturity, including fully automated CI/CD pipelines, robust centralized logging, tracing (correlation IDs), service mesh or service discovery, and a plan for eventual consistency (Outbox pattern, Saga pattern).
items.trigger: proposing service decomposition or distributed ownership
items.remediation: Verify the team has infrastructure automation in place. If correlation IDs, telemetry dashboards, and transaction outbox patterns are not yet implemented, pause decomposition and keep the modules inside a unified codebase first.

items.title: Monolith first is a valid architecture decision
items.body: As outlined in Martin Fowler's 'MonolithFirst' design guidance, starting directly with microservices for a new system carries a high risk of incorrect boundary choices. It is significantly easier to refactor, split, and extract microservices from a well-structured modular monolith once the domain patterns and real-world bottlenecks are understood.

items.title: Document architecture decision drivers
items.body: Every major architectural decision must be documented in an Architectural Decision Record (ADR). The ADR must detail: 1) Context & drivers (technical or business problems). 2) Options considered (with pros/cons). 3) Selected option and justification. 4) Consequences (what we lose, operational requirements). 5) Revision triggers (when to re-evaluate).
items.trigger: creating an ADR, comparing modular monolith, hexagonal architecture, microservices, event-driven design, or serverless

description: Security guidance for web risks, attack simulation, multi-party authorization, dispatch authentication, payload validation, fine-grained RBAC, audit trails, anomaly detection, rate limiting, and kill-switch patterns — with cross-language library references for every major ecosystem. Aligned with OWASP Top 10, NIST Cybersecurity Framework, MITRE ATT&CK, and CIS Controls.
query_hint: Recall this domain for threat modeling, injection/XSS, rate limits, red-team simulation, dual/m-of-n authorization, FROST threshold signatures, Keycloak/SPIFFE authentication, CAP payload validation, OpenFGA/Casbin RBAC, immudb audit trails, Wazuh anomaly detection, and feature-flag kill switches. Also recall for OWASP Top 10, NIST, MITRE ATT&CK, and CIS Controls.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Block SQL injection paths
items.body: All database queries must use bound, parameterized placeholders or secure ORMs/query builders. Never build dynamic SQL strings by concatenating user-controlled text, request headers, or query parameters. Treat custom sorting, pagination, and migration scripts as high-risk execution points.
items.trigger: building database queries or search filters
items.remediation: Replace raw SQL string concatenation with query parameters (`?` or named binds). If dynamic column names are required for sorting/filtering, validate them against a strict allow-list before executing the query.

items.title: Prevent XSS by default
items.body: Treat all user input as untrusted. When rendering strings in HTML templates or javascript, use escaping utilities by default. If rich-text HTML rendering is required, sanitize the content using a secure, actively maintained sanitizer (e.g. DOMPurify) and restrict allowed tags. Implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) header.
items.trigger: rendering user-controlled content in browser, email, docs, or admin panels
items.remediation: Do not bypass default template escaping (e.g., avoid `v-html` in Vue or `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` in React unless inputs are pre-sanitized). Validate all user-supplied links to prevent `javascript:` protocol payloads.

items.title: Apply rate limiting to abuseable endpoints
items.body: Protect all authentication, registration, password-reset, billing, search, and file-upload endpoints from abuse, credential stuffing, and Denial of Service (DoS) attacks. Implement tiered rate-limiting using Token Bucket or Sliding Window algorithms based on client IP, account ID, and API keys.
items.trigger: exposing public or semi-public endpoints
items.remediation: Add middleware to throttle requests. Respond with HTTP status 429 (Too Many Requests) and include a `Retry-After` header. Configure alerts for sudden spikes in 401/403/429 status codes.

items.title: Run red-team style validation
items.body: Simulate real attack flows on features pre-release: 1) Map input paths and trust boundaries. 2) Attempt privilege escalation (accessing user B's resource from user A's account). 3) Attempt rate limit bypasses. 4) Inject malicious payloads (SQL, XSS, Path Traversal, SSRF). 5) Validate that logs do not leak secrets or PII during attack attempts.
items.trigger: pre-release security review or high-risk feature review

items.title: Defend against script kiddies and APT-style persistence
items.body: Harden the runtime environment to mitigate automated scanners and patient attackers. Ensure minimal container images (distroless) are used, restrict process privileges, run applications as non-root, automate dependency patch pipelines, rotate secrets regularly, and set up alert notifications for anomalous file system edits.
items.trigger: reviewing production exposure, admin capabilities, secrets, or infrastructure
items.remediation: Audit open ports. Run dependency vulnerability scans (`npm audit`, `cargo audit`, Snyk). Ensure secrets are loaded via environment variables or a vault rather than committed to source control.

items.title: Use OWASP cheat sheets as review anchors
items.body: Conduct security code reviews by mapping findings to concrete OWASP Cheat Sheets (e.g., REST Security, Session Management, SQL Injection Prevention). Cross-reference architecture with the NIST CSF 2.0 framework (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) and CIS Controls v8 technical benchmarks to satisfy security compliance.
items.trigger: performing application security review or creating remediation tasks

items.title: Threat model trust boundaries before coding
items.body: Before writing code for security-sensitive domains (auth, payment, admin actions), conduct a threat modeling session (e.g. STRIDE). Identify assets, entry points, actors, and where trust transitions from validated to unvalidated. Document how threats are mitigated in the code design.
items.trigger: adding authentication, authorization, payments, file upload, admin actions, webhooks, public APIs, or integrations
items.remediation: Create a diagram showing trust boundaries and data flow. List the security controls applied at each boundary (e.g. JWT validation, payload schema validation, network firewall rules).

items.title: Enforce dual / multi-party authorization (four-eyes, m-of-n) on high-impact actions
items.body: High-impact or irreversible actions (critical alerts, production deploys, financial transfers, bulk deletions) must require independent approval from at least N parties before executing. Key pattern: prefer network-service implementations for security-critical controls — they centralize logic in one audited place instead of reimplementing per language, which is how inconsistencies and bypasses creep in. Three implementation tiers: 1) POLICY LAYER (any language) — Open Policy Agent encodes 'needs N approvers' as policy; Temporal or Camunda (BPMN gates) manages the durable approval workflow; HashiCorp Vault provides a battle-tested Shamir-quorum unsealing reference for m-of-n unlock. 2) IN-PROCESS RBAC ENGINE — Casbin has first-class ports for almost every ecosystem: Go (casbin), Python (pycasbin), Node (node-casbin), Java (jCasbin), Rust (casbin-rs), .NET (Casbin.NET), PHP, and Ruby. 3) CRYPTOGRAPHIC LAYER — For true dual-control where no single compromised key fires the action, use threshold signatures: the FROST spec has implementations across ecosystems (frost crates in Rust; frost-ed25519 in Go; ZF maintains cross-language references). The dispatch fires only after k-of-n parties have independently signed.
items.trigger: designing dispatch systems, critical alert pipelines, deployment gates, financial authorization flows, bulk data deletion, or admin privilege escalation
items.remediation: Replace single-approver workflows with an explicit multi-party gate. Implement OPA or Casbin policies that validate the approver set before the action executes. For cryptographic-grade control, implement threshold signatures with FROST — fire only after k-of-n parties have signed. Log every approval decision (approver identity, timestamp, action hash) to an immutable audit store.

items.title: Apply strong, layered authentication on sensitive dispatch and admin paths
items.body: Authentication on high-risk paths must use multiple factors and short-lived credentials. Prefer the network-service tier for centralized, audited identity management. INFRA (any language): Keycloak, the Ory stack (Kratos/Hydra/Oathkeeper), Zitadel, or Authentik as your OIDC/MFA/passkey provider — your app speaks OIDC so language is irrelevant. For mTLS / 'only verified workloads call dispatch': SPIFFE/SPIRE (CNCF) gives every service a cryptographic SVID identity replacing shared secrets; Linkerd or Istio meshes provide the same mTLS with less wiring. IN-PROCESS libraries by concern: JWT verification — golang-jwt (Go), PyJWT/Authlib (Python), jose (Node/TS), Nimbus JOSE + JWT / SmallRye JWT (Java/JVM), built-in + jose-jwt (.NET), jsonwebtoken / josekit (Rust). OIDC client — coreos/go-oidc (Go), Authlib (Python), openid-client (Node), built-in OIDC (Java), built-in (.NET), openidconnect crate (Rust). WebAuthn relying-party — go-webauthn (Go), py_webauthn (Python), SimpleWebAuthn (Node), webauthn4j (Java), Fido2 (.NET), webauthn-rs (Rust). Token TTLs must be minutes, not hours. Reject requests without a valid certificate or SVID at the network perimeter.
items.trigger: building authentication for dispatch paths, admin panels, alert publication, CI/CD gates, or any workload-to-workload communication that triggers irreversible actions
items.remediation: Enable MFA on all human-authenticated admin paths. Issue scoped, short-lived JWTs (claims: specific action, target, expiry in minutes). Use SPIFFE/SPIRE SVIDs for machine-to-machine authentication on dispatch endpoints. Rotate credentials automatically. Validate the OIDC token's audience claim to ensure tokens minted for one service cannot be replayed against another.

items.title: Validate dispatch payload content and structure at the ingestion gate
items.body: Every payload traversing a critical dispatch path must be validated against a strict schema before it is enqueued, stored, or broadcast. Structural validation catches malformed inputs and injection attempts that exploit downstream parsers. Prefer enforcing structure at the wire level with Protocol Buffers or Apache Avro — both generate validators for every major language and make schema-invalid payloads undeserializable by construction. DOMAIN ANCHOR (any language): validate alerts against CAP (Common Alerting Protocol), the OASIS open standard for emergency alerts — a single abstract word fails the required-fields check and never broadcasts. IN-PROCESS JSON Schema validators: ajv (Node/TS), jsonschema (Python), santhosh-tekuri/jsonschema (Go), networknt/json-schema-validator (Java), JsonSchema.Net (.NET), jsonschema crate (Rust). Validate at ingestion, not at consumption. Schema must be versioned and stored in source control alongside the dispatch service.
items.trigger: building dispatch systems, alert publication pipelines, event buses, webhook endpoints, or any boundary where external or semi-trusted data drives outbound actions
items.remediation: Define a JSON Schema or Protobuf/Avro schema for every dispatch message type. Add schema validation as the first middleware step on every ingestion endpoint. Reject and log schema-invalid payloads with structured error details including the failing field path. For alert systems, validate all required CAP fields (identifier, sender, sent, status, msgType, scope, info blocks) before allowing dispatch.

items.title: Enforce fine-grained, least-privilege RBAC — prefer a centralized authz service
items.body: Authorization must be explicit, narrowly scoped, and centrally enforced — never assumed from authentication alone. Prefer the network-service option: OpenFGA (CNCF) or SpiceDB (Authzed) expose Zanzibar-style fine-grained authz over gRPC/HTTP with SDKs for Go, Python, Node, Java, .NET, Rust, and more — this is the right home for 'only the highest role, plus quorum, can send Extreme severity.' For in-process embedding, Casbin covers all major languages (casbin/Go, pycasbin/Python, node-casbin, jCasbin/Java, casbin-rs/Rust, Casbin.NET). For simpler role→permission mappings: Keycloak's built-in RBAC. Regardless of implementation: operators must never inherit dispatch or broadcast permissions by default; privileges must be explicitly granted, scoped to specific resource types and severity levels, and must carry an expiry.
items.trigger: designing role systems, permission checks for dispatch, alert severity gates, admin actions, or any endpoint where user privilege determines action availability
items.remediation: Replace any implicit 'authenticated = authorized' checks with explicit permission evaluation via OpenFGA or Casbin. Model dispatch authorization as: subject X is allowed to perform action Y on resource Z given conditions C. Audit all permission grants on a scheduled review cycle. Restrict the highest-severity dispatch roles to a named, minimal set of identities with MFA required.

items.title: Implement immutable audit trail, anomaly detection, rate limiting, and a feature-flag kill switch
items.body: Four production-grade safety layers required on any high-risk dispatch or broadcast system. Prefer infrastructure-tier implementations (language-agnostic) over per-app reimplementation. 1) TAMPER-EVIDENT AUDIT (any language): immudb for cryptographically verifiable, immutable dispatch logs; sigstore/rekor for transparency-log append-only style. 2) ANOMALY DETECTION / SIEM (any language): Wazuh (open-source SIEM/XDR) or OpenSearch Security Analytics — alert on off-hours dispatch, abnormal volumes, or off-protocol content. Avoid Elastic unless current licensing is confirmed. 3) RATE LIMITING — Gateway tier (any language): Apache APISIX or Kong OSS, so limits hold regardless of app language. In-process by language: bucket4j (Java/Quarkus-native), limiter/sliding-window (Go), slowapi/limits (Python), rate-limiter-flexible (Node/TS), governor (Rust), AspNetCoreRateLimit (.NET). 4) KILL SWITCH: implement as an audited feature flag, not manual service takedown. Unleash (self-hostable, fine-grained targeting) or OpenFeature (CNCF standard) + flagd — a flag-based kill switch toggles in seconds without a deployment, is fully audited, and can be scoped to specific channels or severity levels.
items.trigger: building critical dispatch systems, alert publication, autonomous action pipelines, or any system requiring emergency shutdown capability
items.remediation: Deploy immudb alongside the dispatch service and write a structured log entry for every lifecycle event (created, approved, rejected, sent, failed). Configure Wazuh rules for off-hours and volume anomalies. Integrate gateway-level rate limiting on all dispatch endpoints and add in-process per-identity caps as a second layer. Implement the feature-flag kill switch as the first code path evaluated on every dispatch request — if the flag is off, reject immediately and log.

description: Testing strategy guidance for unit, integration, stress, load, and browser automation coverage. Incorporates ISTQB Guidelines, Cypress Best Practices, Playwright documentation, and Selenium Docs.
query_hint: Recall this domain when planning or reviewing tests, load tooling, browser automation, or release confidence. Also recall for ISTQB, Cypress, Playwright, and Selenium.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Cover business rules with unit tests
items.body: All core business logic, validation rules, calculations, algorithms, and parsers must have comprehensive unit test coverage. Unit tests must be fast, fully deterministic, run in-memory, and have no external network, file, or database dependencies. Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern.
items.trigger: adding or changing logic
items.remediation: Extract business logic into pure, framework-independent functions that can be tested in isolation. Mock external adapters or repositories using interfaces, stubs, or mock frameworks.

items.title: Use integration tests for contracts
items.body: Verify integration boundaries (database persistence, API endpoint contracts, event queue pub/sub, external HTTP requests, and SQL migrations) using integration tests. Test the real boundary where possible instead of mocking it, using local database instances (e.g., via Docker Testcontainers) or controlled local servers.
items.trigger: changing persistence, API boundaries, external integrations, or migrations
items.remediation: Write integration tests that spin up a local DB, execute migrations, insert test data, invoke the code, and assert on database state or API response payloads. Clean up state after every test run.

items.title: Plan stress and load tests by risk
items.body: Define load testing strategies before writing scripts: 1) Identify peak traffic patterns and data size. 2) Set performance budgets (e.g. p95 latency < 200ms). 3) Formulate bottleneck hypotheses (e.g., DB pool size, CPU limits). 4) Choose the appropriate tool (k6, Locust, Vegeta). 5) Define test profiles (load, spike, soak/stability).
items.trigger: testing performance, scaling, or reliability

items.title: Automate user-critical flows with Playwright or Cypress
items.body: End-to-End (E2E) browser automation must cover critical user paths (auth, checkouts, registration, key workflows). To prevent flakiness: 1) Use stable test attributes or text selectors instead of fragile CSS class selectors. 2) Avoid arbitrary time sleeps; wait explicitly for visible DOM states or network requests to complete.
items.trigger: shipping UI changes or critical workflows
items.remediation: Replace `await page.waitForTimeout(2000)` with `await page.waitForSelector('.success-message')` or page routing expectations. Isolate test accounts and seed test data deterministically before running the E2E suite.

items.title: Test pyramid is a risk model
items.body: The classic test pyramid (many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, minimal E2E tests) is a cost-to-confidence model. High-risk, complex logic calls for dense unit tests, while API endpoints and database integrity require thorough integration coverage. Adjust the test mix based on risk profile, not rigid percentage mandates.

items.title: Choose test tooling by failure mode
items.body: Select test tools based on specific failure modes under ISTQB risk-based testing guidelines: 1) Logical/parsing errors -> Unit tests. 2) DB/API contract mismatch -> Integration tests with Testcontainers. 3) Performance/resource leaks -> k6 soak/spike tests. 4) Layout/UI regressions -> Playwright E2E visual tests using trace viewers for debugging.
items.trigger: planning a test strategy or adding release gates

items.title: Make automation deterministic and observable
items.body: Automated tests in CI must be reproducible and clean. Avoid sharing state or database tables between parallel tests without proper isolation. Log query traces, capture screenshots/videos for E2E failures, and mock external API dependencies using deterministic HTTP mock servers (e.g., WireMock) to prevent external service downtime from breaking CI.
items.trigger: writing CI tests, browser tests, integration tests, load scripts, or regression suites
items.remediation: Configure the test runner to clean the database state between tests (e.g., via transactions or truncated tables). Ensure tests use unique IDs or isolation schemas when executed in parallel.

description: Enterprise front-end practices covering framework selection, component design, accessibility (WCAG), IHC usability heuristics, and the Hick-Hyman Law. Based on MDN Web Docs, React/Vue/Svelte Official Documentation, Smashing Magazine, Nielsen Norman Group, and WCAG.
query_hint: Recall this domain for Angular, React, Vue, Svelte, UI component design, accessibility (WCAG), IHC usability heuristics, and Hick-Hyman choice optimization. Also recall for MDN, Smashing Magazine, Nielsen Norman Group, and WCAG.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Choose front-end libraries by product constraints
items.body: Select front-end frameworks (Angular, React, Vue, Svelte) and component libraries (shadcn/ui, Chakra UI, Material UI) by auditing: 1) Initial page load time / Bundle size constraints. 2) Search Engine Optimization (SEO) & Server-Side Rendering (SSR) needs. 3) Team familiarity and design system alignment. 4) Accessibility primitives quality.
items.trigger: choosing UI framework, component library, or design-system tooling
items.remediation: Establish a performance budget. Compare package weights using Bundlephobia. Document UI selection in an ADR and avoid mixing multiple CSS-in-JS or component frameworks in the same project.

items.title: Validate against AI-slop UI
items.body: Reject generic, low-quality generated UIs that feature weak layout hierarchy, confusing visual contrast, lack of hover/focus states, decorative element clutter, poor spacing, or components that don't match actual data models. UI must look deliberate and align with professional design tokens.
items.trigger: reviewing generated UI, mockups, or component implementations
items.remediation: Establish a grid and spacing scale (e.g. 4px/8px grid). Inspect responsive behavior, empty states, and loading skeletons. Ensure every button and interaction has a clear, functional visual feedback state.

items.title: Make accessibility a default constraint
items.body: Accessibility is a core software quality attribute. All user-facing components must support keyboard navigation (logical tab focus order), focus-trapping inside modals, visible focus indicators, screen-reader landmark labels, and appropriate semantic HTML elements before styling.
items.trigger: building forms, navigation, modals, menus, tables, or custom controls
items.remediation: Use semantic primitives first (e.g., `<button>` instead of `<div onclick>`). Use ARIA attributes (`aria-expanded`, `aria-label`, `aria-hidden`) to represent dynamic state changes to screen readers.

items.title: Keep enterprise components predictable
items.body: Enterprise UI components must handle edge cases gracefully: 1) Loading states (skeletons or spinners). 2) Empty states (call-to-actions when data is missing). 3) Error boundaries (preventing page crashes on API errors). 4) Network disconnect / offline-first state. 5) Permission-gated actions (disabled or hidden controls).
items.trigger: building reusable front-end components or pages
items.remediation: Define structured states in components (e.g., `status: 'idle' | 'loading' | 'error' | 'success'`). Render appropriate views for each state and test error boundary recovery.

items.title: Review front-end changes rigorously
items.body: Before merging UI code, review: 1) Cross-browser compatibility. 2) Responsive layout breakpoints. 3) CSS layout performance (avoiding reflows/layout shifts). 4) Bundle size impact of new imports. 5) Lighthouse scores (Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO).
items.trigger: front-end code review or UI acceptance

items.title: Use WCAG 2.2 Level AA as the accessibility baseline
items.body: Ensure compliance with WCAG 2.2 Level AA guidelines. Specifically: 1) SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured: Keyboard focus indicators must not be completely hidden by sticky headers/footers. 2) SC 2.5.7 Dragging Movements: Provide tap or click alternatives for drag/drop. 3) SC 2.5.8 Target Size: Interactive targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels or have equivalent spacing. 4) SC 3.2.6 Consistent Help: Keep help icons/links in the same relative position. 5) SC 3.3.7 Redundant Entry: Auto-fill previously entered data. 6) SC 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication: Support password managers and avoid cognitive tests.
items.trigger: building or reviewing user-facing UI, forms, navigation, modals, tables, dashboards, or generated layouts
items.remediation: Audit authentication flows to ensure credentials can be pasted or autofilled. Test touch targets using devtools emulation. Manually navigate via keyboard to verify focus states are never obscured by sticky banners.

items.title: Validate framework output against product reality
items.body: Whether using Angular, React, Vue, or Svelte, audit compile-time configurations, ensure strict TypeScript check compliance, enforce lint rules, run bundle analysis, and verify that actual API data matches component data structures.
items.trigger: accepting generated UI, composing component libraries, or building enterprise screens

items.title: Apply IHC principles and usability heuristics for user-centered design
items.body: User interfaces must respect Human-Computer Interaction (IHC) principles to ensure usability, learnability, and accessibility. Apply: 1) Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control/freedom, consistency/standards, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility/efficiency, minimalist design, error recovery, help/documentation). 2) Gestalt Principles (proximity, similarity, continuity, closure) to establish visual hierarchy. 3) Fitts's Law to target sizing and placement (interactive elements must be large enough and close enough to minimize target acquisition time). 4) Cognitive Load reduction (avoid clutter, clear mental models).
items.trigger: designing or refactoring user interfaces, navigation layouts, form fields, interaction feedback, or user workflows
items.remediation: Audit the UI against the 10 heuristics. Ensure interactive targets are at least 44x44px. Align related controls visually to respect proximity. Replace complex instructions with recognition-based cues (e.g., icons with clear text labels).

items.title: Optimize decision speed using the Hick-Hyman Law
items.body: The Hick-Hyman Law states that the time it takes for a user to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number and complexity of choices. To prevent choice overload and decision paralysis: 1) Bounded Choices: Limit options presented at any single step (e.g., maximum 5-7 items in navigation, dropdowns, or selection lists). 2) Progressive Disclosure: Hide advanced or secondary options under tabs, collapsible sections, or 'show more' links. 3) Smart Defaults: Pre-select the most common or recommended option to reduce cognitive effort. 4) Chunking: Group related information or actions into distinct, labeled categories.
items.trigger: designing navigation menus, configuration dashboards, settings pages, form choices, onboarding flows, or command lists
items.remediation: Analyze list options and menus. If a list contains more than 7 items, category-chunk or use search-with-autocomplete. Apply progressive disclosure to secondary actions. Pre-populate sensible default choices in forms to expedite user flows.

items.title: Anthropic's Front-End Design Skill (Landing Pages & Portfolios)
items.body: For landing pages and portfolios, do not default to standard overused fonts and white-to-purple gradients. You must pick a distinct and creative artistic direction before writing any code. Force the UI to reflect a unique identity, avoiding generic AI-generated aesthetic tropes. Pick one visual style (e.g., editorial serif, clean Scandinavian, technical dark mode, neo-brutalism) and apply it consistently across layout, spacing, typography scales, and interactive borders.
items.trigger: designing or building landing pages, portfolios, or marketing sites
items.remediation: Select a specific design direction (e.g., Neo-Brutalism, Retro 80s, Clean Scandinavian) and strictly apply its rules (fonts, color palette, borders) throughout the page.

items.title: Taste Preset: Minimalist UI
items.body: When applying a Minimalist UI (best for blogs and news sites), give content room to breathe. Use ample white space, high-contrast monochrome typography, minimal borders, and zero decorative gradients or unnecessary shadows. Ensure line height is 1.6-1.8 for readability, and restrict max-width of content containers to 65-75 characters (around 650px) to prevent eye strain.
items.trigger: designing blogs, news sites, or reading-focused layouts
items.remediation: Increase padding/margins around text blocks, remove decorative backgrounds, and simplify the color palette to black/white/gray and a single accent color.

items.title: Taste Preset: Industrial Brutalist UI
items.body: When applying Industrial Brutalist UI, use a raw, heavy, anti-corporate design style. Use bold, oversized typography, thick solid borders, stark contrasts, and utilitarian web-safe colors (like pure black, raw yellow, or bright blue). Emphasize structural grid lines, layout containers resembling blueprints, and flat drop shadows (e.g. box-shadow: 4px 4px 0px #000).
items.trigger: designing brutalist, raw, or anti-corporate layouts
items.remediation: Replace soft shadows with hard flat dropshadows. Use thick borders on buttons and containers. Avoid rounded corners and soft gradients.

items.title: Taste Preset: Front-End UIUX
items.body: For solid, clean, professional business sites, act as an all-rounder. Prioritize clear visual hierarchy, legible sans-serif typography (e.g., Inter, Roboto), moderate white space, and subtle, cohesive brand colors that inspire trust. Ensure buttons have distinct states (hover, active, disabled) and maintain consistent spacing offsets (using a 4px/8px grid system).
items.trigger: designing standard professional or corporate business sites
items.remediation: Ensure standard navigation patterns (header with logo and links), clear call-to-actions, and consistent spacing systems are applied.

items.title: Taste Preset: Premium Front-End UI
items.body: For luxury fashion brand landing page layouts, use premium aesthetics. Employ serif and elegant sans-serif font pairings (e.g., Playfair Display with Outfit), full-bleed high-quality imagery, ultra-thin dividing lines, and very subtle slow micro-animations. Ensure generous letter spacing (tracking) on headers and generous whitespace around elements.
items.trigger: designing luxury, fashion, or premium high-end layouts
items.remediation: Remove clutter. Use large, dramatic typography paired with a muted or highly deliberate color palette. Implement slow, smooth transitions.

items.title: Shadcn Component Usage for Dashboards
items.body: When building complex, data-rich dashboards and applications, do not generate custom components from scratch. You must act as a rulebook for Shadcn: integrate with the Shadcn MCP to pull professional-grade, accessible components (data tables, select, dialogs, charts) directly from the registry. Ensure all components use TailwindCSS/utility classes that match the established design system tokens.
items.trigger: building functional UI, dashboards, or data-heavy web applications
items.remediation: Replace manually styled data tables and forms with Shadcn UI components. Connect to the Shadcn registry to scaffold standard complex UI blocks.

items.title: Dashboard Layout Strategy
items.body: For dashboard and analytics tools, effectively group data and arrange information on screen so it looks like a real analytics tool, not a cluttered mess. Use card-based layouts with clear hierarchy, align data points to grids, and prioritize the most important metrics (KPIs) at the top left (or top in mobile). Limit the number of primary dashboard widgets to 3-5 to minimize cognitive load.
items.trigger: designing dashboard layouts, analytics views, or control panels
items.remediation: Restructure disorganized data into distinct cards or panels. Apply consistent padding within cards and consistent gaps between them.

items.title: UIUX Pro Max: Industry-Tailored Systems
items.body: Unlike generic instructions, run a search/evaluation mapped to 161 industry categories to automatically select a tailored color palette, font pairing, and layout that directly matches the specific industry use case (e.g., healthcare needs trust-blue and clean sans-serif; gaming needs dark mode and vibrant accents). Enforce specific industry design guidelines (e.g., high trust, playful, data-dense, editorial) based on the target audience.
items.trigger: starting a new domain-specific project or applying global theme variables
items.remediation: Search the target industry in the UIUX Pro Max database. Apply the recommended font-pairing, color scheme, and container styling to match the industry standard.

items.title: GSAP Animation for Smooth Motion
items.body: For storytelling and complex animations, rely on GSAP instead of generic CSS scroll reveals (which often cause janky browser performance). Use proper, smooth browser-optimized motion vectors (transform, opacity) and GSAP ScrollTrigger for performant, sequenced storytelling. Ensure you kill/revert ScrollTrigger instances on component unmount to prevent memory leaks.
items.trigger: adding scroll animations, parallax, or sequenced motion to storytelling pages
items.remediation: Replace simple intersection-observer CSS classes with GSAP animations targeting composite layers. Avoid animating layout properties (width, top, left, margin).

items.title: Mobile App UI Design Principles
items.body: Never simply shrink a desktop website layout down for a mobile viewport. Enforce core mobile principles: keep interactive elements within the 'thumb zone' (bottom and lower sides), maintain consistent spacing, and apply strict font size limitations similar to layouts used by Airbnb or Spotify. Interactive touch targets must be at least 48x48px with adequate spacing.
items.trigger: designing mobile-first layouts, responsive views, or native mobile apps
items.remediation: Move primary navigation and critical actions to a bottom tab bar or lower sheet. Ensure tap targets are at least 48x48px. Cap header font sizes so they do not break wrapping on small screens.

items.title: Material 3 Design for Android
items.body: When designing for Android apps, bake Google's bold, rounded, and expressive Material 3 design system directly into the logic. Use dynamic color theming based on user wallpaper (monet engine), elevated cards with specific tonal values, and standard Material navigation patterns. Shapes must have correct Material 3 corner roundness (e.g., extra large = 28dp for dialogs).
items.trigger: building Android native apps or Material-themed interfaces
items.remediation: Apply Material 3 shape rounding, typography scales, and tonal elevation instead of custom flat styling.

items.title: SwiftUI Liquid Glass for iOS
items.body: When building for iOS, adhere to Apple's native 'liquid glass' aesthetic constraints as defined in Xcode documentation. Use standard iOS blur materials (thinMaterial, ultraThinMaterial), standard SF Pro typography scales, and native inset-grouped list styles. Interactive buttons should use rounded corners (e.g. cornerRadius of 10-12) and native system colors for dynamic dark/light mode support.
items.trigger: building iOS native apps or Apple-themed interfaces with SwiftUI
items.remediation: Replace solid colored backgrounds on navigation bars and tab bars with native transluscent materials. Use SF Symbols.

items.title: Expo Cross-Platform Unified Workflow
items.body: When building cross-platform apps, use Expo workflows to effectively generate a unified navigation and styling codebase for both iOS and Android simultaneously. Rely on Expo Router for universal file-based routing and platform-specific file extensions (.ios.js, .android.js) when divergence is strictly required. Ensure status bar styling, safe area insets, and splash screens are correctly configured for both platforms.
items.trigger: initializing or structuring a React Native / Expo cross-platform project
items.remediation: Use Expo Router for routing. Ensure SafeAreaView is used to protect content from notches. Use platform-specific stylesheets or styling conditions where native differences are critical.

description: Agent-building guidance across orchestration frameworks, SDKs, tool design, evaluation, and production safeguards. Powered by the ReAct Paper, LangChain, CrewAI, Anthropic/OpenAI Cookbooks, and n8n.
query_hint: Recall this domain when designing agents, choosing orchestration frameworks, adding tools, or evaluating agent reliability. Also recall for ReAct, LangChain, CrewAI, OpenAI Cookbooks, and n8n.
author: matheus-walkowicz

items.title: Choose agent frameworks by orchestration needs
items.body: Select agentic platforms (CrewAI, LangChain, Langflow, watsonx Orchestrate ADK, Antigravity SDK, Vercel AI SDK) by matching the complexity of the task: 1) Deterministic chains vs. autonomous routing. 2) State management and short/long-term memory. 3) Streaming capability requirements. 4) Multi-agent communication protocols. 5) Debugging and tracing suite availability.
items.trigger: choosing an agent framework or SDK
items.remediation: Avoid complex multi-agent swarms unless tasks are highly distinct and require specialized prompts/tools. Implement a simple single-agent router or linear pipeline first.

items.title: Ground agents before consequential actions
items.body: Agents must retrieve relevant rules, database constraints, user context, and preferences (grounding) before performing any consequential action (writing files, calling write APIs, executing database mutations, or sending messages). Safe grounding prevents goal drift, hallucination errors, and harmful action execution.
items.trigger: agent is about to perform a consequential action
items.remediation: Call the `recall` tool to fetch relevant rules before invoking a write tool. surface any high-severity blocking rules in the prompt context. Require explicit user confirmation for irreversible or high-impact actions.

items.title: Design tools with narrow contracts
items.body: Agent tools must have strict schemas (JSON Schema), clear typing, narrow scope, explicit error responses, and no side-effects outside their declared contract. Avoid giving agents broad CLI execution tools or generic database query tools; instead, expose high-level, parameterized actions to keep tool calling predictable.
items.trigger: creating MCP tools, SDK actions, or automation APIs
items.remediation: Break down broad tools into single-responsibility API endpoints. Validate all parameters strictly. Return descriptive error strings (e.g., 'File not found at path X') instead of generic system codes so the LLM can self-correct.

items.title: Evaluate agents with scenario suites
items.body: Build a test evaluation suite for LLM agents (LangChain, CrewAI, Antigravity SDK) representing: 1) Happy path executions. 2) Ambiguous user prompts (tests grounding and clarification prompt logic). 3) Tool execution failures (tests self-correction). 4) Security boundary probes (prompt injection, jailbreaks, data leakage). 5) Bounded loop limits to prevent runaway execution cost.
items.trigger: testing or releasing agent workflows

items.title: Log agent decisions for auditability
items.body: All production agent executions must record a structured trace (episodes) of: 1) Initial user goal. 2) Grounded rules and context retrieved. 3) LLM reasoning steps. 4) Tool calls and parameters. 5) Tool output observations. 6) Final outcomes or errors. Structured logs are critical for security audits, debugging, and offline evaluation optimization.
items.trigger: building production or semi-autonomous agents
items.remediation: Implement trace logging middleware. Capture tokens used, execution latency, and step outcomes. Link logs to a user session or transaction ID.

items.title: Use framework docs as integration contracts
items.body: Orchestration libraries and AI SDKs (LangChain, CrewAI, Vercel AI SDK) evolve rapidly. Lock package dependencies, and treat official APIs, memory adapters, and streaming callbacks as formal contracts. Verify schema updates when upgrading packages to prevent runtime tool-calling crashes.
items.trigger: building or upgrading agent workflows, tools, orchestration, memory, streaming, or SDK integrations
items.remediation: Write unit tests for custom tool mapping. Ensure strict typescript schemas for tool arguments. Run smoke tests for LLM streaming and state management on dependency updates.

items.title: Gate agent autonomy by impact
items.body: Classify agent tools by risk profile: 1) Read-only (low risk). 2) Reversible write (medium risk). 3) Irreversible write, spending, or deploying (high risk). Implement strict authorization gates and human-in-the-loop (HITL) triggers as risk increases.
items.trigger: designing production agents, assistants, autonomous workflows, or MCP/SDK tool permissions
